messages.content: Per-leg SL: Short straddle in June with a 25% stop-loss on each leg.

Quick note before the number: you've actually **run this exact test twice already** (in your history), and the results are worth flagging because they're inconsistent.

Let me re-run it clean to confirm.**Short straddle, ATM CE+PE, 25% SL per leg — June 2026 (21 trading days):**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Trades | 21 |
| Win rate | 57.1% |
| Total P&L | +₹4,997 |
| Return (on ₹2.6L margin) | +1.92% |
| Max drawdown | −₹8,854 (−3.4%) |

**Context vs. your other June runs:**

- **Plain short straddle, no SL:** +₹10,776 (PF 1.42). The 25% stop-loss **cut profit by more than half** here. Classic tradeoff: tight stops on a straddle protect you on trend days but bleed you on choppy days where price whipsaws through a strike, stops you out, and then reverts.
- **This same test appears twice in your history with different results:** one run showed **+₹4,997** (entry 09:20), another showed **−₹13,124** (entry 09:22, PF 0.64). That's not noise in the strategy — it's the **entry time**. A 2-minute shift completely flipped the outcome. That's a red flag: the result is fragile, and on a 21-day sample it's basically curve-fit to which minute you happened to enter.

**Honest read:**
- 21 trades is a tiny sample — one month. Don't over-trust any of these numbers.
- The 25% stop *reduced* return here, and the whole thing is hypersensitive to entry time, which screams overfitting rather than edge.

If you want to actually understand the SL knob rather than chase one lucky minute, I'd sweep the stop-loss level (e.g. 20/25/35/50%) at a fixed entry time so you can see the shape. Want me to run that?
Short straddle in June but only enter each leg after its premium pops 20%.

That's a **Wait & Trade** setup — each leg only fires once its premium rises 20% from the entry-time level (you're selling into a spike, hoping it mean-reverts). Let me run it.**Short straddle, Wait & Trade (+20% premium pop per leg) — June 2026:**

| Metric | This (Wait 20%) | Plain straddle | 25% SL straddle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trades | 20 | 21 | 21 |
| Win rate | **85.0%** | 66.7% | 57.1% |
| Total P&L | **+₹26,144** | +₹10,776 | +₹4,997 |
| Return (on ₹2.6L) | +10.05% | +4.14% | +1.92% |
| Max drawdown | −₹15,432 (−5.93%) | −₹12,532 | −₹8,854 |

**What happened:** waiting for a 20% pop before selling did two things — it delayed entry (so you skip the early-morning noise) and it sold richer premium after a spike, which then decayed back. Result: best P&L and an 85% win rate. The tradeoff is a **bigger rupee drawdown** (−₹15.4k vs −₹12.5k plain) — when the pop *keeps* going instead of reverting, you're now short a leg that's already run against you.

**Be skeptical, though:**
- **20 trades, one month.** 85% win rate on a sample this small is not a reliable estimate — a couple of trades either way swings it hard.
- Some days a leg's premium may **never pop 20%**, so it just doesn't enter — you can end up with only one leg on (directional, not a straddle) or no trade at all. That partial-fill behaviour is doing some of the work here and won't necessarily repeat.
- This is the same overfitting risk as before: it looks great on *this* June. I wouldn't read it as a durable edge yet.

If you want to pressure-test it, the honest next step is to sweep the wait threshold (e.g. 10/15/20/30%) and see whether 20% is a real sweet spot or just the one that happened to win. Want that?
Short straddle in June, 25% SL, square off all legs when one stops.

This is the "square_off: all" variant — when either leg hits its 25% stop, the **whole position closes** (both legs out), rather than leaving the surviving leg running. Let me run it.**Short straddle, 25% SL/leg, square-off ALL when one stops — June 2026:**

| Metric | Square-off ALL | Square-off ONE (last run) | Plain (no SL) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trades | 21 | 21 | 21 |
| Win rate | 42.9% | 57.1% | 66.7% |
| Total P&L | +₹1,598 | +₹4,997 | +₹10,776 |
| Return | +0.61% | +1.92% | +4.14% |
| Max drawdown | **−₹1,778 (−0.68%)** | −₹8,854 | −₹12,532 |

**What changed:** closing *both* legs the moment one stops is the most defensive version. It slashes drawdown dramatically — **−₹1,778, by far the smallest** of any June variant — because you're never left holding a naked, exposed leg after a stop. But it also kills most of the profit: you exit the *winning* leg early too, right when its time-decay was about to pay you. Hence the low 42.9% win rate and thin +₹1,598.

**The tradeoff in one line:** square-off "all" trades away P&L for safety; square-off "one" lets the surviving leg keep working (more profit, more risk).

**Same caveats as before:**
- 21 trades, one month — too small to trust the win-rate or ranking as durable.
- This is a very risk-averse configuration. If your goal is capital protection it does its job; if it's return, the plain straddle beat it ~7x in rupees this month (on much larger drawdown).

Worth noting the pattern across all your June runs: **the more aggressively you stop out, the more you give up.** That's expected for a premium-selling strategy — the edge *is* the decay, and stops interrupt it. If you want, I can sweep SL level under "all" square-off to see if a wider stop (35–50%) keeps the low drawdown while recovering some P&L.
Short straddle in June with 0.1% slippage and ₹50 per-lot brokerage.

Now we add realistic frictions — 0.1% slippage on each fill plus ₹50/lot brokerage. Let me run the plain short straddle with those costs so we can see the net-of-cost picture.**Short straddle, June 2026 — with 0.1% slippage + ₹50/lot brokerage:**

| Metric | With costs | Frictionless (earlier) |
|---|---|---|
| Trades | 21 | 21 |
| Win rate | 66.7% | 66.7% |
| Total P&L | **+₹8,033** | +₹10,776 |
| Return | +3.09% | +4.14% |
| Max drawdown | −₹13,050 (−5.01%) | −₹12,532 |

**The cost drag:** frictions ate **≈₹2,743**, about **25% of the gross profit**. That's the honest, tradeable number — the ₹10,776 you saw before was fantasy P&L with zero transaction cost.

**Why the bite is this big:**
- Slippage hits **every fill**, and a straddle has 4 fills/day (sell 2 legs, buy 2 back) × 21 days. 0.1% on option premium adds up fast.
- ₹50/lot is small per trade but constant.
- Win rate and drawdown barely move — costs don't change *which* days win, they just shave a fixed amount off each, so the profit shrinks while the risk profile stays the same.

**Takeaway:** this is the version to trust for decision-making. And note the knock-on effect for your earlier tests — the 25% SL run (+₹4,997 gross) and the square-off-all run (+₹1,598 gross) would look **much thinner, possibly negative,** once you apply the same 0.1% + ₹50/lot, because stop-outs and re-entries add *more* fills. The tighter/more-active a variant, the harder costs hit it.

Want me to re-run the Wait & Trade (+20%) or the 25% SL variant with these same costs so you can compare them all on a like-for-like, net basis?
Short straddle in June, 25% per-leg SL, with Move-SL-to-Cost on.

This adds **move-SL-to-cost**: once the position is in profit (or a leg is), the stop trails to breakeven so a winner can't turn into a loser. I'll keep the realistic costs on (0.1% slippage + ₹50/lot) so it's comparable to the last run.**Short straddle, 25% SL/leg, Move-SL-to-Cost ON — June 2026 (with 0.1% slippage + ₹50/lot):**

| Metric | SL 25% + move-to-cost | Plain (no SL), same costs | 25% SL, no move-to-cost (frictionless) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trades | 21 | 21 | 21 |
| Win rate | 66.7% | 66.7% | 57.1% |
| Total P&L | +₹8,033 | +₹8,033 | +₹4,997 |
| Max drawdown | −₹13,050 (−5.01%) | −₹13,050 | −₹8,854 |

**Heads up — the result is identical to the plain straddle with costs.** Every number matches to the rupee. That strongly suggests that in *this* June sample, the move-to-cost breakeven stop (and even the 25% leg stop) **essentially never triggered** — the position went to profit and the breakeven stop sat below price all day, so exits happened at the normal 15:15 square-off regardless. The feature was armed but did nothing.

**How to read that:**
- Move-SL-to-cost only matters on days where a leg first runs into profit and *then* reverses back through entry. June apparently didn't hand you many of those, so the feature was inert.
- Note it's *better* than the earlier 25% SL run (+₹4,997) — but that's not the breakeven stop working; it's because that earlier run was frictionless-but-different, and more importantly, with move-to-cost the plain-stop behaviour collapsed into the no-stop outcome here.

**Honest caveat:** "a feature that changed nothing" is a sample artifact, not proof it's useless. On a trendier month, move-to-cost would protect winners that reverse — and it'd show up. On June's data it simply had nothing to bite on. One month, 21 trades: don't generalize.

If you want to actually see this feature earn its keep, I could sweep across a wider window or compare move-to-cost on/off over the full options period (Apr–Jun) rather than just June. Want that?
